featuredQuotes.text: My son is finishing fast to just play games like prodigy . He doesn’t work of handwriting at all!! Bring play back

My elementary children have repeatedly been exposed to inappropriate content on their school issued Chromebooks. They are often on websites that we do not allow at home. I am concerned how the screen time at school is affecting them academically.
I believe devices create a distraction and double the work for teachers.
Shows and movies being played without parents consent on screens very frequently.
I’d like to be able to access my child’s screen time per week and history

Screens seem to be used as a babysitter. My child in 4th grade did not get to play on the playground from Christmas break to the end of March because the teachers thought it was too cold and they had them sit inside on screens instead (not technically too cold to be outside per policy)
My 1st grader was shown an inappropriate image that scared her by another student in her class on his Chromebook.

Knox County District 2 Parent.text:
Excessive screen time reducing physical activity and face-to-face interaction
Privacy and data security concerns involving student information
Cyberbullying and inappropriate online behavior among students
Overreliance on technology limiting development of critical thinking and handwriting skills
Exposure to inappropriate or unreliable online…
My biggest concern is that Chromebooks get used in place of peer interactions (like stations) Time where they can be working together independently but instead spend that time doing iready lessons.
It seems to vary teachers to teacher how much time they have on computers. Like different teacher to teacher and that’s very frustrating to me.

Knox County District 4 Parent.text: I am very concerned about screens during indoor recess/finishing early  and screens for math skills, like iReady. Current research shows those programs are not beneficial for student's’ learning, thus a time filler and way for kids who need more challenge to progress.

One of my primary concerns is student access to the internet through school-issued Chromebooks. While I understand the educational benefits of technology, I believe there should be strong filtering systems and ongoing monitoring in place to help ensure students are not inadvertently exposed to inappropriate content.

I would love to see more meaningful no -screen work offered for early finishers or when there is extra time in the classroom. Ongoing projects and enrichment that pique their interests that can be worked on in the fringe moments for example. I think technology at this age has more potential for harm than good.

In our school, any children who are not in tier 2 or 3 small learning groups, are using chrome books to work on independent learning during this time. I do not believe this is the best use of time for my children who are not currently able to focus independently on productive work time.

There have been whisperings of Tcap testing being completely computerized next year. As a parent of a rising 3rd grader who struggles with test taking anxiety, my concern is that standardized testing on computers penalizes students for typing speed rather than content mastery.

I worry that my daughter’s learning experience is less impactful on devices. Watching lessons or referencing content online doesn’t seem to support her learning and I’m concerned the lack of book/paper/pencil work undermines her ability to practice and build good study skills.

Knox County District 5 Parent.text: We are okay with some screen time & use screens at home as well. However, we are deeply concerned on testing on screens. Our children are performing significantly lower on screen testing than traditional methods. Their teachers have reassured us to not worry and that our children are on target.

Kids play academic games and rush through coursework to get to the reward. Spelling is atrocious when having to use pen and paper because teachers have them write essays on Google Docs so they turn on spellcheck and don’t pay attention to what actually needs to be fixed.

It is my belief that there has been an overreliance on and overindulgence in the use of technology on the part of adults in our elementary school. I also believe faculty are not communicating with one another about the use of screens at different points in the school day, so nobody is fully aware of how much screens are being utilized across the entire day.

Knox County District 8 Parent.text: I know adults use devices at work every day. It is also our responsibility as adults to teach children how to use these devices for work and educational means. This does not need to begin until after age twelve to thirteen. There is no reason for a toddler or young child to need screen time.

Knox County District 1 Parent.text: Specifically for elementary school the screen time is too much. My son had about an hour of just computer time a day, in addition to screen use for instruction, practice and tests. During this hour he was working alone on either math or ELA but often that time could be spent watching videos or playing a high dopamine reward game.
While technology certainly has a place in education, I believe the current level of screen use is becoming a hindrance to meaningful learning and development for my girls who are in K and 2nd.

Knox County Parent.text: Most of the school work in grades 6-8 has been done on Chromebook. It should NOT be a replacement for work done on pencil and paper and it is difficult for parents to see student work once it us submitted. It's hard to know what my kid needs to work on if I am unable to see his work.

There is just no reason that such young age children all need their own chrome book. It is very well established how bad screen time is for our children so I don’t know why we are providing such opportunities to our young elementary grades.

We are told from a young age that screen time hurts them. I was horrified when indound out my Kindergartener waa going to be given a screen. Why? 
It take the content control from the parents. It is not what school should be about.
Concerned about what filters Knox Co uses as my my child (and others I’ve come to find out after sharing my experience) have seen inappropriate things
I just don’t think it’s necessary. They can earn games after a certain number of lessons completed and I really don’t like the idea of them playing games, “educational” or not.

Knox County District 7 Parent.text: I just know my children are spending more time on their devices than necessary for a learning tool. They have told me stories about watching Nerf videos on YouTube. They have way too much time.

Knox County District 3 Parent.text: We’re too addicted to screens as it is, I’d prefer it not be used to replace teaching time or as a reward.
It is not developmentally appropriate for elementary age students to be on the screens for the amount of time they are.
Screens create regression and lacks to diversify processing, it builds intolerance around patience and uncertainty.

Knox County District 6 Parent.text: My son is finishing fast to just play games like prodigy . He doesn’t work of handwriting at all!! Bring play back

District 2.text:
Excessive screen time reducing physical activity and face-to-face interaction
Privacy and data security concerns involving student information
Cyberbullying and inappropriate online behavior among students
Overreliance on technology limiting development of critical thinking and handwriting skills
Exposure to inappropriate or unreliable online…
My biggest concern is that Chromebooks get used in place of peer interactions (like stations) Time where they can be working together independently but instead spend that time doing iready lessons.
It seems to vary teachers to teacher how much time they have on computers. Like different teacher to teacher and that’s very frustrating to me.

One of my primary concerns is student access to the internet through school-issued Chromebooks. While I understand the educational benefits of technology, I believe there should be strong filtering systems and ongoing monitoring in place to help ensure students are not inadvertently exposed to inappropriate content.

I would love to see more meaningful no -screen work offered for early finishers or when there is extra time in the classroom. Ongoing projects and enrichment that pique their interests that can be worked on in the fringe moments for example. I think technology at this age has more potential for harm than good.

In our school, any children who are not in tier 2 or 3 small learning groups, are using chrome books to work on independent learning during this time. I do not believe this is the best use of time for my children who are not currently able to focus independently on productive work time.

There have been whisperings of Tcap testing being completely computerized next year. As a parent of a rising 3rd grader who struggles with test taking anxiety, my concern is that standardized testing on computers penalizes students for typing speed rather than content mastery.

I worry that my daughter’s learning experience is less impactful on devices. Watching lessons or referencing content online doesn’t seem to support her learning and I’m concerned the lack of book/paper/pencil work undermines her ability to practice and build good study skills.

The screens seem to be a band-aid for the help teachers have been needing since way before 2020. We just want more time with our amazing teachers or other wonderful personnel. And no more time with unnecessary games and videos that are not even allowed at our home.

Screen time was used for indoor recess, “brain breaks”, centers- virtually all day in different capacities. This includes shows/videos that the teacher would play. It was also an incentive for good behavior.

My kids have very limited screen access at home and so when they have the opportunity to use at school they jump at the chance and rush through their work to get to them, choose them during indoor recess, and miss out on peer to peer connection and  the beauty of boredom and creativity bc of them

My children were both able to access the adult YouTube at school. We do not allow them to watch YouTube at home and monitor all screen use strictly. They also use Chromebooks for indoor recess and centers. There is also no communication about tv shows that are shown and how often- and tv shows were shown very frequently.

Screens seem to be used as a babysitter. My child in 4th grade did not get to play on the playground from Christmas break to the end of March because the teachers thought it was too cold and they had them sit inside on screens instead (not technically too cold to be outside per policy)

I am honestly not sure how much my child is on a screen but I do know they take brain break to play games online and I don't think that actually gives their brains a break. They watch movies and YouTube and even a classmate was exposed to porn by googling a sexual term on wikipedia.
They are addictive, kids play non educational games on them all day. They cause too much distraction and do not add value.

The data and research is clear that children do not retain information and learning from screens. Paperand pencils and books are far better for their developing brains and ability to retain information.

As both a child of the 90s and a teacher who began teaching in the early 2010s, I’ve had the unique experience of seeing educational technology evolve from an occasional classroom enhancement into a daily, individual experience for students.
Please read the research and look at the data on the effect of screens on young children and adolescents. The data is clear. Screens do not belong in the hands of our children.

The kids frequently use devices at school as free time when they finish their work early and when rainy and they can’t go out for recess. They should not be replacing physical activity and social interactions.
There’s a reason schools in Silicon Valley do not allow screens in the classroom. The people that created this technology know the negative effects that can have on the developing mind of a child.
The amount of time spent on screens at school is very alarming. Kids are on screens various times throughout the day instead of learning from a teacher. Tests should not be given on computers.
Not only is the Chromebook a screen but teaching is done on a screen. Kids are essentially watching a Power Point presentation all day long.
I think it is absurd we are testing children in Kindergarten on computers - and other grades.  But I was appalled this past year when my child was tested with a Chromebook.

While I understand that screens might need to be used for assessments, I strongly believe that screens should not be used for explicit teaching OR for recreational purposes (indoor recess, breaks, etc.)
My oldest child rushes through his work so he can use his Chromebook and play Prodigy. I wish that there wasn’t an incentive to finish work.
I understand that devices can play an important role in education. I worry about the brain breaks and indoor recess screen time.
I believe devices create a distraction and double the work for teachers.
Our children have their entire adulthood to be stuck at a computer screen- let’s let them enjoy childhood and appreciation for traditional learning environments
Shows and movies being played without parents consent on screens very frequently.
I’d like to be able to access my child’s screen time per week and history
